message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: Cross-judge scores are not comparable, and the benchmark now also has a successor (DRB II). Any number quoted for a system without naming its evaluator is untrustworthy — this is the trap that produced both the 48.88 and the 52.65 errors.
DETAIL: Repo news, 11 May 2026: official evaluator switched Gemini-2.5-Pro → GPT-5.5 (FACT pipeline → GPT-5.4-mini), chosen by alignment with human judgments on a 50-task × 4-DRA annotated subset against a human inter-annotator agreement baseline of 68.78% (GPT-5.5 71.82 overall / PAR 73.00 / OPC 89.70 / FAP 65.35 / FAS 59.23, beating Gemini-3.1-Pro 70.58 and Claude-Opus-4-7 70.11). Two leaderboards were maintained in parallel during a dual-acceptance window to 31 May 2026; after 1 June only the GPT-5.5 board is maintained. The 48.88 figure POLARIS was previously burned by is the STATIC table still sitting on deepresearch-bench.github.io (gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch, old judge) — that page is stale and should be treated as poisoned. ADORE's 52.65 is the same class of error one generation later. Also note DeepResearch Bench II shipped 6 Feb 2026 (arXiv 2601.08536) with a different evaluation focus, though DRB continues to be maintained.
SYSTEM: RACE-judge-itself
SOURCE GIVEN: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Reproduce verbatim any News/Updates section, any statement about which model is the RACE judge / evaluator, any mention of GPT-5.5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, evaluator switch, dual leaderboards, human agreement baselines, and the leaderboard table with scores (especially any 48.88 or 52.65). Also any mention of DeepResearch Bench II.

content.content:
# DeepResearch Bench: Key Information

## Overview
DeepResearch Bench is a comprehensive evaluation framework for Deep Research Agents, featuring **100 PhD-level research tasks** across 22 domains. The benchmark employs two complementary evaluation methods: RACE (for report quality) and FACT (for information retrieval accuracy).

## Latest Updates (May 11, 2026)

**Evaluator Migration**: The official RACE evaluator has transitioned from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation. Testing on 200 human-annotated articles showed:

> "All three candidates exceed this baseline by 1.3–3 points; **GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS**"

The human inter-annotator agreement baseline measured **68.78%**.

**GPT-5.5 Performance Metrics:**
- Overall: **71.82**
- PAR: **73.00**
- OPC: 89.70
- FAP: 65.35
- FAS: **59.23**

**Transition Timeline**:
- Now – May 31, 2026: Dual acceptance for both legacy (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and new (GPT-5.5) evaluators
- By June 1, 2026: Full migration to GPT-5.5 leaderboard

## Related Developments
DeepResearch Bench II was released February 6, 2026, with distinct evaluation focus while the original benchmark continues development.

content.text: Let me get the raw file directly rather than trust a summarizer.

15:# ✨ News 16:+ [11 May 2026] 🎯 **Official Evaluator Switched to GPT-5.5**: Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = **68.78%**). All three candidates exceed this baseline by 1.3–3 points; **GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS**. We are adopting it as the new RACE evaluator (with **GPT-5.4-mini** for the FACT pipeline). Results are displayed on **two separate leaderboards** so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator. 27: > - **By 1 June 2026 (full migration)**: For the results reported in the original DRB paper, we will re-evaluate them under GPT-5.5 and migrate them to the new leaderboard automatically. Upgraded evaluation infrastructure with **Gemini-2.5-Pro** for RACE and **Gemini-2.5-Flash** for FACT evaluation (since superseded — see top of News). All raw research articles and evaluation scores are now available on our [**Hugging Face Leaderboard**](https://huggingface.co/spaces/Ayanami0730/DeepResearch-Leaderboard) for comprehensive analysis and comparison. 116:**View Latest Leaderboard**: Visit our [**DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard**](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard) for real-time updated evaluation results, detailed comparative analysis, and raw data. 127:1. **A temporary key with access to GPT-5.5** 194:| Backend | RACE judge (`Model`) | FACT judge (`FACT_Model`) | 196:| openrouter | `openai/gpt-5.5` | `openai/gpt-5.4-mini` | 197:| openai | `gpt-5.5` | `gpt-5.4-mini` |

15	# ✨ News

All three candidates exceed this baseline by 1.3–3 points; **GPT-5.5 wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS**. We are adopting it as the new RACE evaluator (with **GPT-5.4-mini** for the FACT pipeline). 37	    - [DeepResearch Bench II](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08536): Evaluates DRA-generated reports with 9,430 fine-grained binary rubrics (information recall, analysis, presentation) derived from expert-written articles.
38	    - [Wiki Live Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01590): A live benchmark that uses Wikipedia Good Articles as expert-level references, with fine-grained criteria for writing quality and factual verifiability.
39	    - [WildGraphBench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02053): Benchmarks GraphRAG on long, heterogeneous documents with 1,100 questions spanning single-fact QA, multi-fact QA, and section-level summarization.
40	  - **Agents**:
41	    - [A-RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03442): An agentic RAG framework that exposes hierarchical retrieval interfaces (keyword search, semantic search, chunk read) to the model for adaptive multi-granularity retrieval.
42	    - [FS-Researcher](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01566): A file-system-based dual-agent framework (Context Builder + Report Writer) that scales deep research beyond the context window via a persistent knowledge base.
43	
44	  **If you want to evaluate your deep research agent** please see the leaderboard submission requirements below and contact us at dumingxuan@mail.ustc.edu.cn and imlrz@mail.ustc.edu.cn.
45	+ [18 July 2025] 🎉 We have established a partnership with **AGI-Eval** platform. DeepResearch Bench is now available on [**AGI-Eval**](https://agi-eval.cn/evaluation/detail?id=67), providing a more convenient evaluation interface for researchers and practitioners to test their deep research agents.
46	+ [15 July 2025] ⚡️⚡️ **Major Update**: Added comprehensive evaluation of **Kimi-Researcher**, **Doubao-DeepResearch**, and **Claude-Researcher**. Upgraded evaluation infrastructure with **Gemini-2.5-Pro** for RACE and **Gemini-2.5-Flash** for FACT evaluation (since superseded — see top of News). content.text: Nearly everything in the DETAIL matches the README verbatim. Now checking the two remaining sub-claims (the 48.88 static page, and 52.65).

input.command: grep -o -i -E ".{300}48\.88.{80}" /tmp/drb_site.html | head -3; echo "---UPDATED?---"; grep -o -i -E "(last[ -]?updat|2025|2026)[^<]{0,60}" /tmp/drb_site.html | sort -u | head

python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
h=open('/tmp/drb_site.html',encoding='utf-8',errors='ignore').read()
for m in re.finditer(r'48\.88',h):
    s=re.sub(r'\s+',' ',re.sub('<[^>]+>',' ',h[max(0,m.start()-700):m.start()+120]))
    print(">>>",s[-350:],"\n")
EOF

Based on the search results, I found information about the ADORE system and the 52.65 RACE score on DeepResearch Bench:

ADORE achieved a score of 52.65 on the public DeepResearch Bench, with component scores of Comprehensiveness: 52.22, Insight: 54.37, Instruction Following: 51.11, and Readability: 52.18, placing it at rank #1 on the leaderboard.

On the Internal Enterprise Benchmark, ADORE achieved a RACE score of 64.11, with component scores of Comprehensiveness: 65.91, Insight: 66.76, Instruction Following: 60.35, and Readability: 61.95, demonstrating a 14.11% improvement in quality over human baselines for enterprise tasks.

content.text:
Every element of the DETAIL checks out against the primary source, verbatim.

**Primary source: `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md`** (fetched raw via curl, not just via a summarizing fetch — saved at `/tmp/drb_readme.md`, 282 lines). The `# ✨ News` section, lines 15–46:

- **[11 May 2026] "Official Evaluator Switched to GPT-5.5"** — confirmed. Cause given: "Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro". Selection method: "the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = **68.78%**)". FACT pipeline → **GPT-5.4-mini**. All exactly as claimed.
- **Candidate table (line 20-22)** — exact match: GPT-5.5 71.82 / 73.00 / 89.70 / 65.35 / 59.23; Gemini-3.1-Pro 70.58; Claude-Opus-4-7 70.11.
- **Dual-acceptance window to 31 May 2026, two separate leaderboards, GPT-5.5-only after 1 June** — confirmed (lines 26–27): "Results are displayed on **two separate leaderboards** so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator… After 1 June, Gemini-2.5-Pro acceptance ends and only the GPT-5.5 leaderboard is maintained going forward." That sentence is also the benchmark maintainers themselves asserting the claim's core point (comparability holds *within* an evaluator, not across).
- **DRB II, 6 Feb 2026, arXiv 2601.08536, different evaluation focus, DRB still maintained** — confirmed verbatim (line 33).
- Config section (lines 194–197) independently corroborates: RACE judge `openai/gpt-5.5`, FACT judge `openai/gpt-5.4-mini`.

**The 48.88 "poisoned page" sub-claim** — confirmed independently. `https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/` still serves a static table where "Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research 48.88" leads, with prose "Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrated leading overall performance (48.88)". The page contains zero mention of GPT-5.5 or the evaluator migration and its only date strings are 2025 — i.e. it is the old-judge table, exactly as the claim characterizes it.

**ADORE 52.65** — corroborated: arXiv 2601.18267 reports 52.65 on public DRB (Comp 52.22 / Insight 54.37 / IF 51.11 / Read 52.18), a Jan-2026 paper, therefore necessarily under the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro judge and quoted without naming it. Same class of error, one generation later, as the claim says.

Two caveats that do not undermine the verdict but are worth carrying: (1) the README notes the GPT-5.5 leaderboard was "still under construction — expected to launch within a week" as of 11 May, so a *published* GPT-5.5 number for any given system may not exist yet; (2) the 71.82/70.58/70.11 numbers are judge-vs-human *alignment* scores, not DRB leaderboard scores — the claim states this correctly, but it is an easy thing to later misquote.

Sources: [DRB README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md), [stale static leaderboard](https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/), [ADORE paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.18267)

input.reasoning:
Verified against the primary source by fetching the raw README with curl (not just a summarizing fetch), then independently checking the two sub-claims the README cannot cover.

1) README News section (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/README.md), entry "[11 May 2026] Official Evaluator Switched to GPT-5.5" confirms EVERY specific in the DETAIL verbatim:
- Trigger: "Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro".
- Method: "the human-annotated subset (50 tasks x 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = 68.78%)".
- FACT pipeline moved to GPT-5.4-mini; config table later in the README corroborates (RACE judge `openai/gpt-5.5`, FACT judge `openai/gpt-5.4-mini`).
- Candidate table exact match: GPT-5.5 71.82 overall / PAR 73.00 / OPC 89.70 / FAP 65.35 / FAS 59.23; Gemini-3.1-Pro 70.58; Claude-Opus-4-7 70.11.
- Migration plan: dual-acceptance window "Now - 31 May 2026", "Results are displayed on two separate leaderboards so the rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator", and "After 1 June, Gemini-2.5-Pro acceptance ends and only the GPT-5.5 leaderboard is maintained going forward." This last line is the maintainers themselves asserting the claim's core point about cross-judge non-comparability.
- DRB II: "[6 Feb 2026] DeepResearch Bench II Release", arXiv 2601.08536, "has a different evaluation focus from DRB; DRB will continue to be maintained and updated" — matches the claim precisely.

2) The 48.88 "stale/poisoned page" sub-claim is confirmed independently: https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/ still serves a static table led by "Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research 48.88" with prose "Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrated leading overall performance (48.88)". The page has no mention of GPT-5.5 or the evaluator migration and its only date strings are 2025 — it is the old-judge table, exactly as characterized.

3) ADORE 52.65 is corroborated: arXiv 2601.18267 reports 52.65 on the public DeepResearch Bench (Comprehensiveness 52.22 / Insight 54.37 / Instruction-Following 51.11 / Readability 52.18). As a Jan-2026 paper it necessarily used the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro judge and quotes the number without naming the evaluator — the same class of error, one generation later.

Two caveats that do not change the verdict but should be carried forward: (a) the README states the GPT-5.5 leaderboard was "still under construction — expected to launch within a week" as of 11 May 2026, so a published GPT-5.5 number for any given system may not exist yet; (b) 71.82/70.58/70.11 are judge-vs-human ALIGNMENT scores, not DRB leaderboard scores — the claim states this correctly but it is easy to misquote later.
